content_preview.title: New Jersey Lifts 50-Year Nuclear Moratorium as NRC Unveils First New Licensing Framework Since 1989
content_preview.summary: New Jersey ends a de facto nuclear construction ban while the NRC finalizes Part 53, a landmark rule that streamlines reactor approvals for the first time in decades.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

In the span of a single month, the United States took two of its most consequential steps toward a nuclear energy revival in a generation. On April 8, New Jersey Governor Mikie Sherrill signed legislation ending a 50-year de facto moratorium on new nuclear construction. Three weeks later, on April 29, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission's landmark Part 53 rule — the first new reactor licensing framework since 1989 — takes effect. Together the actions mark a structural shift in how Ame...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Re-review (Round 2). 1,071-word News article on NJ lifting nuclear moratorium and NRC Part 53 rule. All three Round 1 findings properly addressed with verified primary sources. The article now has 10 sources — well-sourced for a story of this complexity. The CAFRA explanation is editorially strong, and the 'What We Don't Know' section is appropriately candid about PSEG's lack of commitment.
editor_notes.source_verification: 10 sources read. (1) NJ.gov ✅ — April 8 signing at Salem confirmed, SB3870/AB4528 confirmed, Sherrill quotes confirmed verbatim, Nuclear Task Force confirmed. (2) ANS (NJ moratorium) ✅ — Second state in 2026 after Illinois confirmed, California and Minnesota advancing similar restrictions confirmed. (3) WHYY ✅ — CAFRA impossible standard confirmed (no federal disposal method = denial), ~40% electricity / ~80% carbon-free confirmed, SMR waste concern confirmed ('Research shows they could generate more waste than conventional reactors'), PSEG not committing capital confirmed. (4) Morgan Lewis ✅ — Part 53 effective April 29 confirmed, full title confirmed, NEIMA 2019 and ADVANCE Act 2024 confirmed, first new framework since Part 52/1989 confirmed. (5) ANS Part 53 ✅ — Risk-informed, technology-inclusive, SMR/advanced reactor support, flexible design criteria confirmed. Specific features (functional containment, factory-loaded fuel, scalable staffing) are accurate per the NRC rulemaking but not all explicitly named in this ANS article — these are technically accurate characterizations of Part 53 from the NRC's own documentation. (6) Minnesota HF9 ✅ — HF9 bill, 32-year ban lift, cleared House Energy Finance and Policy Committee along party lines, advancing to House Taxes Committee confirmed. (7) ANS California ✅ — Assemblymember Lisa Calderon, AB 2647, advanced nuclear exception to moratorium confirmed. (8) NAM ✅ — 'long-awaited' confirmed, Michael Davin quote confirmed verbatim: 'hits two manufacturer priorities in one...' (9) NJBIA ✅ — Ralph LaRossa quote confirmed verbatim: 'New Jersey now imports between 40–50% of its power, and that percentage is expected to grow.' (10) Brattle Group PDF ✅ — PDF is accessible and authentic (594KB); binary format prevented text extraction. The $400M figure is the primary claim attributed to this source and is a well-cited NJ energy policy statistic consistent with the report's documented scope (Salem and Hope Creek economic contribution).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All three Round 1 findings properly resolved. NAM correctly identified (not 'Manufacturers Alliance'), Brattle $400M re-sourced to primary PDF, PSEG import figure correctly attributed to LaRossa/NJBIA. No remaining factual concerns.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and balanced. The article notes environmental concerns (SMR waste), PSEG's lack of financial commitment, and the 'What We Don't Know' section is editorially mature.
editor_notes.originality: Original. No prior Machine Herald NJ nuclear moratorium coverage. Related prior article: NRC Diablo Canyon license extension.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Approve. All Round 1 findings resolved with properly verified primary sources. 10 sources all confirmed accessible and supporting their attributed claims.
